skill.name: memory-safety-patterns
skill.description: Implement memory-safe programming with RAII, ownership, smart pointers, and resource management across Rust, C++, and C. Use when writing safe systems code, managing resources, or preventing memory bugs.
skill.summary: Implement memory-safe programming with RAII, ownership, smart pointers, and resource management acro...
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found command, filesystem, network, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance patterns in SKILL.md. Review shows these are educational code examples, compiler/debugger commands, memory-safety terminology, or documentation links rather than executable skill behavior. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: Thread Spawn Examples Are Educational
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Rust thread::spawn calls as process spawning. These lines are inside fenced Rust examples that demonstrate safe shared ownership and data-race prevention, not operating system command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged calls are in Markdown code fences and create Rust threads, not child processes. No user-controlled command string or shell invocation is present.

low_findings.title: Diagnostic Shell Commands Require User Intent
low_findings.description: The skill includes compiler and analysis commands for AddressSanitizer, Valgrind, Miri, and ThreadSanitizer. They are documentation examples and are not executed by the skill, but users should run them only on trusted local projects.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly presented in a fenced bash block under Debugging Tools. They are legitimate developer tools and contain no download, exfiltration, or destructive operations.

low_findings.title: Local File Write Appears in C Cleanup Example
low_findings.description: The fopen call writes to log.txt inside an illustrative C resource-management example. It is not invoked by the skill, but copied code would create or overwrite a local file if used unchanged.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write is directly visible and exists inside a code example. The impact is limited because the marketplace skill is Markdown guidance, not runnable automation.

low_findings.title: Documentation Links Flagged as Network Indicators
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are references to official C++, Rust, and LLVM documentation. The skill does not fetch these URLs or send data to them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only as Markdown resource links. There is no network client code, credential handling, or request construction near these lines.

low_findings.title: Memory-Safety Terms Misclassified as Dangerous Patterns
low_findings.description: Weak-crypto and reconnaissance alerts appear to be triggered by terms such as double-free, destructor, destroy, socket, and debugging tools. These are relevant memory-safety concepts, not evidence of cryptographic misuse or reconnaissance behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms are embedded in explanations of memory bugs and resource cleanup. No cryptographic algorithm choice, host scanning, or network probing logic is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copyable Local Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide provides shell commands for compilers and debugging tools. This is normal for a systems-programming skill, but marketplaces should label them as user-run examples rather than automated actions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and copyable, so they are operational guidance. They are still low risk because they run local developer tools without external endpoints or destructive flags.

content.value_statement: Memory bugs can cause crashes, data corruption, and security vulnerabilities in systems software. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through practical RAII, ownership, and cleanup patterns.
actual_capabilities: Explains memory bug categories and prevention strategies.
Shows RAII patterns for files, locks, and rollback in C++.
Compares C++ smart pointers, Rust ownership, and C cleanup patterns.
Demonstrates bounds checking and thread-safety techniques.
Lists debugging tools for memory and data-race issues.
limitations: Does not run static analysis or memory sanitizers automatically.
Does not verify that examples compile in every project environment.
Does not replace expert review for unsafe Rust, C, or C++ code.
Focuses on patterns, not full application architecture.

use_cases.title: Review Systems Code
use_cases.description: Find likely ownership, lifetime, cleanup, and bounds-checking problems before code review.

use_cases.title: Choose Safer Patterns
use_cases.description: Select RAII, smart pointers, Rust borrowing, or C cleanup paths for a resource-management task.

use_cases.title: Teach Memory Safety
use_cases.description: Explain memory bugs and prevention patterns with concrete examples across common systems languages.

prompt_templates.title: Explain the Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain which memory-safety pattern fits this code and why. Focus on ownership, cleanup, and lifetime risks.

prompt_templates.title: Improve a Function
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this function for memory-safety risks. Suggest RAII, smart pointer, borrowing, or cleanup changes with brief reasoning.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Language Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare C, C++, and Rust approaches for this resource-management problem. Recommend the safest practical pattern for our constraints.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Concurrent Code
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this concurrent systems code for data races, lifetime issues, and unsafe sharing. Include the safest synchronization or ownership model.

output_examples.input: A C++ class opens a file and closes it manually in several return paths.
output_examples.output: The result identifies missing cleanup paths, recommends RAII, and explains how a destructor ties the file lifetime to object scope.

output_examples.input: A Rust function clones shared state across worker threads.
output_examples.output: The result checks whether Arc, Mutex, RwLock, or atomics fit the access pattern and explains the data-race implications.

output_examples.input: A C function allocates two resources and returns early on error.
output_examples.output: The result proposes a single cleanup path and explains how each resource should be released exactly once.

best_practices: Prefer scope-bound cleanup for files, locks, memory, and sockets.
Make ownership transfer explicit in function names, types, and documentation.
Use sanitizers and compiler warnings to verify safety assumptions.
anti_patterns: Do not copy raw pointers when ownership is unclear.
Do not scatter cleanup logic across many return paths.
Do not use unsafe Rust or unchecked indexing without a documented reason.

faq.question: Does this skill execute code?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples for memory-safe systems programming.

faq.question: Which languages does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Rust, C++, and C, with comparisons to garbage-collected languages.

faq.question: Can it find memory leaks automatically?
faq.answer: No. It can suggest patterns and tools, but it does not run analyzers.

faq.question: Is it useful for unsafe Rust?
faq.answer: Yes, but it should support expert review rather than replace it.

faq.question: Why are shell commands included?
faq.answer: They show common local debugging tools such as sanitizers, Valgrind, and Miri.

faq.question: Can teams use it for code review?
faq.answer: Yes. It helps reviewers ask focused questions about ownership, lifetimes, cleanup, and data races.
